common_name: Wild Banana (Balbis Banana, Saging na Saba)

planting.note: Plant corm divisions (pups/suckers) in spring after last frost in full sun in rich, well-drained, moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Musa balbisiana is more cold-tolerant and drought-resistant than the common dessert banana and is one of the parents of most cultivated banana varieties.

directions: Plant suckers in deeply cultivated, rich, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Musa balbisiana is more drought-tolerant than Musa acuminata and can tolerate brief dry spells once established, but performs best with regular moisture. Musa balbisiana (wild banana) is one of the two ancestral wild banana species from which virtually all cultivated banana and plantain varieties worldwide derive — along with Musa acuminata, it contributed the 'B' genome to many banana cultivars (Cavendish bananas are triploid AAA; plantains are typically AAB or ABB — the B refers to M. balbisiana's genetic contribution). The species produces seedy, starchy fruits that require cooking and are eaten in Asia, but its primary horticultural significance in Western gardens is as an ornamental banana and as an ancestor of cultivated types. It produces striking, large, paddle-shaped leaves on a 10-to-15-foot pseudostem — similar in appearance to the plantain but slightly smaller — and is among the most cold-hardy Musa species, surviving temperatures to 22–25°F when well-mulched, making it more reliable as a zone 8 ornamental landscape plant than most banana species. In zone 9, it grows as a near-permanent perennial specimen.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Banana

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost in peat pots to minimize transplant shock; celosia dislikes root disturbance. Direct sow outdoors after soil warms to 70°F. In zone 9, start in March for spring planting or in August for fall color.

directions: Start in peat pots indoors or direct sow after soil warms to 70°F in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Celosia argentea var. cristata (cockscomb) is the most dramatically formed of the celosias, producing a single, large, brain-like, fan-shaped, fasciated (abnormally flattened and broadened) flower head that can reach 4–6 inches wide in deep crimson, scarlet, orange, yellow, pink, or cream — the odd, velvet-textured, convoluted crest is one of the most unusual flower forms in cultivation and has been grown in gardens since ancient times in Asia, where it is used as a temple offering plant. Cockscomb thrives in hot, humid conditions and is outstanding in Gulf South gardens from late spring through frost; it is heat-tolerant and drought-resistant once established. Dwarf types ('Chief' series, 'Jewel Box' series) grow 6–10 inches with proportionally large flower heads; tall types can reach 18–24 inches. All celosias make exceptional dried flowers — harvest the crests before the first frost and hang upside down to dry; they hold color for months.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers

planting.note: Direct sow thickly in warm soil (70°F+) in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; cultivated purslane is one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant summer salad greens and is an outstanding vegetable for zone 9 gardens where most salad plants bolt in summer heat.

directions: Scatter seeds on warm, bare soil and press in lightly — seeds need some light to germinate; thin to 4–6 inches or harvest as a cut-and-come-again crop. Portulaca oleracea sativa (culinary or golden purslane) is the large-leaved, succulent, upright or spreading culinary form of the common purslane weed, distinguished from the weed by larger, more succulent, often golden-yellow to golden-green leaves and a more upright, less spreading habit. It is one of the most nutritious and heat-tolerant summer vegetables — extraordinarily high in omega-3 fatty acids (the highest of any leafy vegetable), vitamin C, and beta-carotene — with a mildly tart, slightly mucilaginous, fleshy texture and pleasant sour flavor used raw in salads, stir-fried, or added to soups and stews throughout Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and Mexican cuisines. In zone 9, culinary purslane can be grown from April through October, thriving in summer heat and drought when all other salad plants fail — it is the definitive summer salad green for hot, dry gardens. Common golden purslane cultivars include 'Goldberg' (large, golden-yellow leaves) and 'Gruner' (green leaves, more upright).
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Summer Vegetables

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost or direct sow after soil warms in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; cranberry hibiscus is an exceptionally fast-growing tropical shrub used for both edible leaves and ornamental foliage — the deep burgundy-red leaves are among the most striking of any edible plant.

directions: Start seeds indoors or direct sow after frost; plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; cranberry hibiscus grows rapidly in warm conditions and begins producing harvestable leaves within 6–8 weeks of planting. Hibiscus acetosella (cranberry hibiscus or African rosemallow) is a tropical West African edible shrub producing dramatically beautiful, deeply lobed, maple-leaf-shaped, deep burgundy-red to maroon foliage on rapidly growing plants that reach 4–6 feet by summer's end — among the most ornamental of all edible plants. The young leaves are harvested and eaten raw in salads (slightly tart, like sorrel, with a bright red-purple color that bleeds into dressings) or cooked as greens in the same manner as its close relative roselle (H. sabdariffa); the tart, cranberry-like flavor comes from hibiscus acids and anthocyanins. Small, lavender-pink flowers bloom in late summer and are also edible. In zone 9, it grows as an annual (or short-lived perennial) from March through November; in zones 10–12, it persists year-round. The rich leaf color and tropical texture make it as ornamental as it is edible — outstanding as a focal point or screening plant in the vegetable garden.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Vegetables
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Edibles

common_name: Southern Pea (Cowpea, Black-eyed Pea, Field Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Southern peas are one of the most important and widely grown summer vegetables in the Gulf South, thriving in heat and humidity while fixing atmospheric nitrogen — a dual-purpose food and cover crop.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after soil warms to 65°F; thin to 4–6 inches. Vigna unguiculata (southern pea, cowpea, or black-eyed pea) is the most heat-tolerant major legume in North American horticulture and an absolute staple of Southern gardens and cuisine — encompassing black-eyed peas, crowder peas, cream peas, pink-eye purple hull peas, lady peas, and Iron-and-Clay peas in a single species with hundreds of named varieties grouped by pod color, seed markings, and intended use. All share the capacity to fix atmospheric nitrogen (greatly enriching soil when the crop residue is turned under), exceptional heat and drought tolerance, and thrive in the humid, hot conditions of Gulf Coast summers when other legumes fail. Fresh 'green' (immature) southern peas are shelled and cooked like shell beans; dry peas are stored for long-term use. 'California Black-eye' (classic black-eyed pea), 'Pinkeye Purple Hull,' 'Iron-and-Clay' (cover crop type), 'Mississippi Silver,' and 'Texas Cream' are widely grown named varieties. In zone 9, southern peas can be planted from April through August for continuous production.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Southern Peas
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cowpea
